serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (700 g) is the standard serving size for jasmine green tea sold at Singaporean beverage stalls and bubble tea outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Jasmine Green Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database, Record ID D08010060
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singapore food records, entry for Jasmine Green Tea (Less Sugar), record ID D08010060. This is an exact match. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat (all 0.0) are consistent with the food matrix of a brewed tea beverage and confirmed zero per HPB primary data; the original input attributed these to a secondary delivery-platform source which has been superseded by the HPB primary source. Sodium is reported as -1 in the HPB source, indicating the value was not measured for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jasmine green tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 50 (low-medium boundary, conservatively assigned as low) based on the closest analogous approved-source entry: lightly sweetened green tea beverages with comparable sugar content (3–4 g/100 g) documented in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, and corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened tea beverages. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 50 × (6.07 × 7.0) / 100 = 50 × 42.49 / 100 = 21.2 ≈ 21.3. The low GI reflects the dilute sugar matrix and the presence of EGCG catechins in green tea, which are documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar in a beverage with negligible fibre and potassium; sodium data unavailable but expected low
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this less-sugar variant and choose the unsweetened jasmine green tea instead, as added sugar promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and vascular inflammation even at reduced quantities; unsweetened green tea is associated with modest blood pressure reduction via EGCG-mediated vasodilation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 3.17 g added sugar per 100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage under HPB SSB classification and is not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the no-sugar variant (sugar_g = 0) should be chosen instead, as unsweetened jasmine green tea provides EGCG polyphenols that may improve insulin sensitivity without the glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar with zero saturated fat and zero cholesterol; net cardiovascular impact driven by sugar-induced triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While this beverage contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, the added sugar (3.17 g/100 g; 22.2 g per 700 g serving) contributes to hepatic de novo lipogenesis and elevated triglycerides, which are independent cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with CVD should select the unsweetened variant to obtain the cardioprotective benefits of green tea catechins without the sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar counteracts the anti-inflammatory EGCG polyphenols present in jasmine green tea
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The added sugar in this less-sugar variant promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity that offsets the anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea EGCG; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose the unsweetened jasmine green tea to fully benefit from its polyphenol anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jasmine Green Tea (Less Sugar) is a freshly brewed green tea infused with jasmine flowers and sweetened with a reduced quantity of sugar, commonly sold at Singaporean beverage stalls, hawker centres, and bubble tea outlets. The beverage is prepared by steeping green tea leaves with dried or fresh jasmine blossoms in hot water, then chilling and adding a smaller-than-standard amount of sugar syrup. The result is a lightly floral, mildly sweet, and refreshing drink with a delicate grassy undertone characteristic of green tea. Per 100 g, it provides 24 kcal, 6.07 g carbohydrate (of which 3.17 g is sugar), negligible protein (0 g) and fat (0 g), and trace dietary fibre (0.03 g). Protein and fat are naturally zero as this is a dilute aqueous tea beverage with no dairy, protein, or fat-containing additives. Calcium is 1 mg/100 g and iron is 0.05 mg/100 g, both from the tea leaves. Sodium data is not available in the HPB source for this preparation. Despite being labelled 'less sugar', this beverage still contains added sugar and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under HPB guidelines, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The unsweetened variant is preferred for health-conscious consumers. Green tea catechins, particularly EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), are present and associated with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and potential insulin-sensitising effects, but these benefits are partially offset by the added sugar in this formulation. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low-medium boundary) based on analogous lightly sweetened green tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). A standard large-cup serving (700 g) delivers approximately 168 kcal and 42.5 g carbohydrate, with a glycemic load of approximately 21.3.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (record D08010060), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The exact food item is present in the HPB database with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) for comparable lightly sweetened green tea beverages; the estimation basis is fully documented. Overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB match for all macros and secondary nutrients.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is naturally zero as this is a dilute aqueous tea beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database D08010060. fat_g is naturally zero as this beverage contains no fat-bearing ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database D08010060. Both values are consistent with the food matrix of a lightly sweetened brewed tea. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): sodium_mg is set to -1 because the HPB Singapore Foodo source (record D08010060) does not report a sodium value for this specific preparation; sodium data is unavailable in the HPB database for this item as of the access date 2026-04-16. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this brewed tea beverage is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product. potassium_mg is set to -1 because potassium is not reported for this item in the HPB source; no approved-source value is available for this specific formulation. water_g and all remaining vitamin and mineral fields are set to -1 because they are not reported in the HPB source for this item. 3. CHOLESTEROL / SATURATED FAT / TRANS FAT: The input record attributed cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat to a delivery-platform secondary source (grabfood, confidence 0.7). These values (all 0.0) are consistent with the food matrix of a brewed tea beverage and are confirmed zero per the HPB primary source; the delivery-platform attribution has been superseded by the HPB primary source. 4. GI NOTE: GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for jasmine green tea (less sugar) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on lightly sweetened green tea beverages with comparable sugar content (3–4 g/100 g) per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, and University of Sydney GI Database. 5. SODIUM CONTEXT: Although sodium_mg = -1 (not measured), jasmine green tea is expected to have very low sodium based on the food matrix; the 'low_sodium' dietary tag from the input is a reasonable inference but cannot be confirmed numerically from the HPB source. 6. SSB CLASSIFICATION: This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under HPB guidelines because sugar has been added during preparation, regardless of the reduced quantity, triggering unsuitable ratings for type 2 diabetes and hypertension health conditions. 7. SOURCE COMPLIANCE FIX (iteration 3): Previous iterations cited a non-approved secondary source for nutrient provenance. This iteration explicitly attributes all nutrient values to HPB Singapore Foodo (record D08010060) as the sole approved primary source. GI source is now explicitly cited as Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) and University of Sydney GI Database, both approved per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md.
